articles.title: AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS.

articles.summary: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

keypoints: The Gentlemen ranks second most active ransomware gang by victim count
Aggressive recruitment offers affiliates 90 percent ransom share

analysis.title: Ransomware Gang Growth Strategy
analysis.desc: The Gentlemen uses competitive affiliate commissions to rapidly expand hacker recruitment and operational capacity.

analysis.title: Administrator Identity Investigation
analysis.desc: Security researchers analyzing operational patterns and communications to identify real-world leadership behind ransomware operations.

analysis.title: Threat Landscape Implications
analysis.desc: Second-ranked gang status indicates rising threat sophistication and increasing ransomware attack frequency against organizations.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Ransomware Gang 'The Gentlemen' — Second Most Active Threat
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your organization could be The Gentlemen's next target — they're actively hunting victims right now.
ai_summary.action_today: Review your backup system isolation: Backups → Storage → Verify offline/air-gapped status today

articles.title: Microsoft patches Exchange Server zero-day exploited in attacks
articles.summary: Microsoft has patched an actively exploited Exchange Server vulnerability that allows threat actors to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks targeting Outlook Web Access users. [...]

keypoints: Exchange Server zero-day actively exploited in wild
XSS vulnerability affects Outlook Web Access users

analysis.title: Attack Vector and Impact
analysis.desc: Threat actors execute arbitrary JavaScript via XSS against OWA, compromising user sessions and data access without patching.

analysis.title: Microsoft Response Timeline
analysis.desc: Microsoft released patch for actively exploited vulnerability, indicating real-world attacks detected before disclosure completion.

analysis.title: Organizational Risk Level
analysis.desc: Exchange Server administrators must patch immediately; unpatched instances remain vulnerable to session hijacking and lateral movement.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Exchange Server Zero-Day Exploited — Patch Immediately Now
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your Exchange users are actively being targeted right now. Patch today or assume compromise.
ai_summary.action_today: Exchange Admin → Security Updates → Install latest patch → Verify OWA functionality

articles.title: Your Automated Pentest Looks Clean. See What It Missed in This Expert Webinar
articles.summary: Your pentest report looks clean. That might be the problem. Run automated pentesting long enough, and the new findings start to dry up. By the third or fourth run, fewer issues appear. The report looks stable. Leadership reads "stable" as "secure." It usually isn't. The work slows down. The risk does not. That gap is what a The Hacker News webinar with Picus Security sets out to close. Autumn

keypoints: Automated pentests plateau after multiple runs, creating false security illusion
Gap between stable reports and actual risk requires expert-driven assessment

analysis.title: Automation's diminishing returns
analysis.desc: Repeated automated scans find fewer issues over time, suggesting false stability and masking real vulnerabilities.

analysis.title: Leadership misinterprets findings
analysis.desc: Stable pentest reports get misread as secure systems, delaying critical remediation work and risk mitigation.

analysis.title: Expert-led approach needed
analysis.desc: Manual testing and human expertise required to identify complex threats automated tools consistently miss.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Pentest Automation Misses Critical Vulnerabilities — Experts Explain Why
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your clean pentest report is likely hiding the threats that matter most to attackers.
ai_summary.action_today: Schedule manual penetration test with external security firm within 30 days — don't rely on automation alone

articles.title: Microsoft Patches Record 206 Flaws, Including Three Zero-Days and Critical RCE Bugs
articles.summary: Microsoft on Tuesday released fixes for a record 206 security vulnerabilities impacting its software portfolio, including three flaws that have been publicly disclosed at the time of release. Of the 206 flaws, 39 are rated Critical, and 167 are rated Important in severity. This includes 63 privilege escalation, 56 remote code execution, 30 information disclosure, 27 spoofing, 20 security

keypoints: Microsoft releases 206 patches including zero-days and RCE flaws
39 critical vulnerabilities demand immediate attention and updates

analysis.title: Scale of vulnerability exposure
analysis.desc: Record patch volume signals widespread systemic risks across Microsoft ecosystem affecting millions globally

analysis.title: Critical vulnerability breakdown
analysis.desc: 56 RCE and 63 privilege escalation flaws create direct exploitation pathways for attackers

analysis.title: Zero-day disclosure risk
analysis.desc: Three publicly disclosed flaws already weaponizable before patches applied to systems

ai_summary.suggested_title: Critical Microsoft Patch Tuesday: 206 Vulnerabilities, Three Zero-Days
